subject: Python
description: Core Python concepts for QA Automation interviews

topics.title: Variables & Data Types
topics.keywords: int float str bool none type isinstance dynamic typing
topics.definition: A variable is a named container storing a value in memory. Python is dynamically typed — no type declaration needed; Python infers type at runtime.
topics.code:
age       = 25         # int
pi        = 3.14       # float
name      = "Pooja"   # str (immutable)
is_active = True      # bool
data      = None      # NoneType

# Inspect type
print(type(age))               # <class 'int'>
print(isinstance(name, str))   # True

# is vs == (interview classic)
a = [1, 2]; b = [1, 2]
print(a == b)   # True  — same value
print(a is b)   # False — different objects in memory
topics.tip: Python uses dynamic typing — type is determined at runtime. Use isinstance() over type() because it respects inheritance. For None checks always use 'is None', never '== None'.

topics.title: Mutable vs Immutable
topics.keywords: mutable immutable list dict string tuple change memory default arg trap
topics.definition: Mutable objects can be changed in-place after creation. Immutable objects create a new object on any change. This directly affects how Python passes data to functions.
topics.code:
# Immutable: int, float, str, bool, tuple
s = "hello"
# s[0] = "H"  →  TypeError!

# Mutable: list, dict, set
lst = [1, 2, 3]
lst[0] = 99   # works fine

# ⚠️ Famous mutable default arg trap
def add_item(item, lst=[]):   # WRONG
    lst.append(item)
    return lst

add_item("a")   # ["a"]
add_item("b")   # ["a", "b"]  ← bug! shared list

# Correct — use None as default
def add_item(item, lst=None):
    if lst is None: lst = []
    lst.append(item)
    return lst
topics.tip: Never use a mutable object (list, dict) as a default argument — it is shared across all calls. Use None as the default and create a new list inside the function.

topics.title: Control Flow — if / for / while
topics.keywords: if elif else for while range enumerate zip ternary break continue loop
topics.definition: Python control flow uses indentation instead of braces. Key tools: enumerate() for index+value, zip() for parallel iteration, range(start,stop,step) for number sequences.
topics.code:
# if-elif-else
score = 75
if   score >= 90: print("A")
elif score >= 60: print("B")   # ← this runs
else:            print("Fail")

# Ternary one-liner
status = "pass" if score > 60 else "fail"

# enumerate — index + value together
for i, val in enumerate(["a", "b", "c"]):
    print(i, val)   # 0 a | 1 b | 2 c

# zip — iterate two lists in parallel
names   = ["login", "signup"]
results = ["pass",  "fail"]
for n, r in zip(names, results):
    print(f"{n}: {r}")

# range(start, stop, step)
list(range(0, 10, 2))   # [0, 2, 4, 6, 8]
topics.tip: enumerate(), zip(), and range(start, stop, step) come up constantly in coding rounds. Know all three cold.

topics.title: List
topics.keywords: list append pop remove sort slice index comprehension ordered mutable duplicate
topics.definition: Ordered, mutable, indexed collection. Allows duplicates. Most commonly used Python data structure.
topics.code:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]

# Indexing & slicing
fruits[0]        # "apple"
fruits[-1]       # "cherry" (last)
fruits[0:2]      # ["apple", "banana"]
fruits[::-1]     # reversed list

# Common methods
fruits.append("mango")    # add to end
fruits.insert(1, "grape") # add at index
fruits.remove("banana")   # remove by value
fruits.pop()              # remove & return last
fruits.sort()             # in-place sort
sorted(fruits)            # returns new sorted list

# List comprehension (interview favourite)
squares = [x**2 for x in range(5)]   # [0, 1, 4, 9, 16]
evens   = [x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0]
topics.tip: sort() modifies in place; sorted() returns a new list — that distinction often comes up. List comprehensions are more Pythonic and faster than equivalent for-loops.

topics.title: Dictionary
topics.keywords: dictionary dict key value get update items keys values KeyError json insertion-ordered
topics.definition: Insertion-ordered (Python 3.7+), mutable key-value pairs. Keys must be unique and immutable. O(1) average lookup time.
topics.tip: Always use .get(key, default) instead of direct bracket access to avoid KeyError. In QA, dicts are ideal for test parameter maps — each scenario's inputs stored by name.

topics.title: Set
topics.keywords: set unique intersection union difference membership O(1) frozenset deduplicate
topics.definition: Unordered, mutable, unique elements only. O(1) membership check. Supports mathematical set operations like union, intersection, difference.
topics.tip: Sets give O(1) membership check vs O(n) for lists. In QA I use set difference to find which test cases ran vs expected — one clean line.

topics.title: Tuple
topics.keywords: tuple immutable ordered hashable unpack named coordinates swap
topics.definition: Ordered, immutable collection. Faster than lists. Can be used as dictionary keys (lists cannot). Best for fixed data like coordinates, config, DB rows.
topics.code:
coords = (10.5, 20.3)
single = (42,)        # trailing comma required for single element

# Unpacking — very common
x, y = coords
print(x)   # 10.5

# Pythonic variable swap — no temp variable!
a, b = 1, 2
a, b = b, a   # a=2, b=1

# Named tuple — readable alternative
from collections import namedtuple
Point = namedtuple("Point", ["x", "y"])
p = Point(3, 4)
print(p.x, p.y)   # 3  4
topics.tip: Tuple vs list — use tuple when data should not change. Tuples are hashable so they can be dict keys; lists cannot. a, b = b, a uses tuple packing/unpacking to swap without a temp variable.

topics.title: Class & Object
topics.keywords: class object init self constructor instance method attribute blueprint POM page object model classmethod staticmethod
topics.definition: A class is a blueprint. An object is an instance of that blueprint. __init__ is the constructor — runs when object is created. self refers to the current instance.
topics.tip: In QA, classes are the foundation of Page Object Model — each page is a class, locators are instance variables, actions are methods. This makes tests readable: login_page.login(user, pass) rather than raw Selenium calls everywhere.

topics.title: Inheritance & super()
topics.keywords: inheritance super parent child BasePage extends reuse POM override
topics.definition: A child class inherits all attributes and methods from a parent class. super() explicitly calls the parent's method. Promotes code reuse — no duplication.
topics.tip: BasePage holds shared utilities — wait_for_element, take_screenshot, scroll_to. Every page class inherits it, zero duplication. super().__init__() ensures the parent constructor runs and the driver is set up.

topics.title: Decorators
topics.keywords: decorator wrapper functools wraps retry log timing cross-cutting
topics.definition: A function that wraps another function, adding behaviour before or after it runs without modifying the original. Syntax sugar for func = decorator(func).
topics.tip: Decorators add cross-cutting behaviour — logging, retry, timing — without touching the function. @pytest.fixture and @pytest.mark.parametrize are decorators. Always use @functools.wraps so the decorated function keeps its original name.

topics.title: pytest — Fixtures & Parametrize
topics.keywords: pytest fixture conftest parametrize scope session function module class yield data driven
topics.definition: @pytest.fixture = injectable setup/teardown injected by parameter name. @pytest.mark.parametrize = data-driven testing — run one test with many input sets.
topics.code:
import pytest

# Session-scoped — browser launches ONCE for all tests
@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def browser():
    from playwright.sync_api import sync_playwright
    with sync_playwright() as p:
        b = p.chromium.launch(headless=True)
        yield b      # setup done — test runs here
        b.close()    # teardown — always runs

# Function-scoped — fresh page per test (default)
@pytest.fixture
def page(browser):
    p = browser.new_page()
    yield p
    p.close()

# Data-driven — 3 test cases from 1 function
@pytest.mark.parametrize("user,pwd,expected", [
    ("admin",  "pass123", "dashboard"),
    ("user1",  "wrong",   "error"),
    ("",       "",        "required"),
])
def test_login(page, user, pwd, expected):
    page.goto("/login")
    page.fill("#user", user)
    page.fill("#pass", pwd)
    page.click("#submit")
    assert expected in page.url
topics.tip: Fixture scope is the most important thing to get right: session = browser launches once, function = fresh page per test. Wrong scope = either test pollution or huge slowdown. Default: function for page, session for browser.

topics.title: conftest.py — Shared Fixtures
topics.keywords: conftest shared fixtures automatic discovery injection no import needed
topics.definition: Special pytest file — fixtures defined here are automatically available to all tests in the same directory and subdirectories. No import needed in test files.
topics.tip: conftest.py is pytest's dependency injection. Tests declare what they need by parameter name — pytest resolves and injects. Put all shared fixtures here: browser, base URL, auth tokens, DB connections.

topics.title: API Testing with requests
topics.keywords: requests API GET POST status code json response assert authorization bearer token session
topics.definition: Python requests library for HTTP API testing. Always validate: status code, response body fields, and schema structure.
topics.code:
import requests

# GET with auth header
resp = requests.get(
    "https://api.example.com/users/1",
    headers={"Authorization": "Bearer TOKEN"}
)
assert resp.status_code == 200
data = resp.json()          # response as dict
assert data["name"] == "Pooja"

# POST with JSON payload
payload = {"name": "Test User", "role": "QA"}
r = requests.post(
    "https://api.example.com/users",
    json=payload,           # auto sets Content-Type: JSON
    headers={"Authorization": "Bearer TOKEN"}
)
assert r.status_code == 201
assert "id" in r.json()

# Session — reuse headers across calls
session = requests.Session()
session.headers.update({"Authorization": "Bearer TOKEN"})
session.get("/users")
session.post("/orders", json={...})
topics.tip: Always validate three things: status code, response body field values, and schema structure using jsonschema. Use requests.Session() for multi-step API tests to share auth headers and cookies.

subject: Playwright
description: Playwright architecture, locators, and automation patterns

topics.title: How Playwright Works
topics.keywords: playwright architecture CDP WebSocket browser chromium firefox webkit protocol direct no driver
topics.definition: Playwright communicates directly with browsers via CDP/WebSocket — a persistent pipe with no HTTP overhead per command. No separate driver binary needed.
topics.code:
# Layer stack
# Your test (Python)
#   ↕ WebSocket (CDP)
# Browser process (Chromium / Firefox / WebKit)

from playwright.sync_api import sync_playwright, expect

with sync_playwright() as p:
    browser = p.chromium.launch(headless=False)
    context = browser.new_context()   # isolated like incognito
    page    = context.new_page()

    page.goto("https://example.com")

    # Semantic locators — resilient to UI changes
    page.get_by_label("Username").fill("admin")
    page.get_by_label("Password").fill("pass")
    page.get_by_role("button", name="Login").click()

    # Auto-retry assertions — no explicit wait needed
    expect(page).to_have_url("https://example.com/home")
    expect(page.get_by_text("Welcome")).to_be_visible()

    browser.close()
topics.tip: Playwright auto-waits for elements to be visible, enabled, stable, and not obscured before acting. No WebDriverWait or time.sleep() needed. The trace viewer records every step with DOM snapshots for debugging.

topics.title: Locator Types
topics.keywords: locator get_by_role get_by_label get_by_text get_by_placeholder get_by_test_id CSS XPath selector
topics.definition: Playwright has semantic locators that target elements the way users see them — by role, label, text. These are more resilient than CSS or XPath.
topics.code:
# Prefer semantic locators (top to bottom priority)
page.get_by_role("button", name="Submit")    # ARIA role
page.get_by_label("Email address")           # form label
page.get_by_placeholder("Enter email")       # placeholder
page.get_by_text("Welcome back")             # visible text
page.get_by_alt_text("Company logo")         # img alt
page.get_by_test_id("submit-btn")            # data-testid

# CSS and XPath (fallback when needed)
page.locator("#submit")
page.locator(".btn-primary")
page.locator("//button[@type='submit']")

# Chaining locators
page.locator(".modal").get_by_role("button", name="OK")

# Strict mode — throws if multiple match
page.locator(".btn")   # throws if > 1 element matches
topics.tip: Playwright's strict mode throws if a selector matches more than one element — catches bugs Selenium would silently ignore. get_by_role() and get_by_label() survive UI refactors if semantics stay the same.

topics.title: Network Interception & Mocking
topics.keywords: route intercept mock API network abort fulfill request response
topics.definition: Playwright can intercept any network request — mock API responses, block resources, or modify requests. Built-in, no proxy needed.
topics.code:
# Mock an API endpoint
page.route("**/api/products", lambda route: route.fulfill(
    status=200,
    content_type="application/json",
    body='[{"id": 1, "name": "Mocked Product"}]'
))

# Block images to speed up tests
page.route("**/*.{png,jpg,jpeg,gif,svg}",
           lambda route: route.abort())

# Intercept and modify request
def handle(route, request):
    headers = {**request.headers, "X-Test": "true"}
    route.continue_(headers=headers)

page.route("**/api/**", handle)

# Listen to responses
page.on("response", lambda r:
    print(r.status, r.url) if "/api/" in r.url else None
)

page.goto("https://shop.example.com")
topics.tip: Route mocking is perfect for testing edge cases without a real backend — 500 errors, empty states, slow responses. Block images and CSS in regression tests to cut run time by 30-40%.

topics.title: Trace Viewer & Debugging
topics.keywords: trace viewer debug screenshot video record on failure CI
topics.definition: Playwright has built-in trace recording — captures every action with DOM snapshot, network activity, console logs, and screenshots. Replayed in a visual viewer.
topics.code:
# Enable tracing in conftest.py
@pytest.fixture(scope="function")
def page(browser):
    context = browser.new_context()
    context.tracing.start(
        screenshots=True,
        snapshots=True,
        sources=True
    )
    page = context.new_page()
    yield page
    # Save trace on test failure
    context.tracing.stop(path="trace.zip")
    context.close()

# playwright.config (pytest.ini or pyproject.toml)
# [tool.pytest.ini_options]
# addopts = "--tracing=retain-on-failure"

# Open trace in browser
# playwright show-trace trace.zip
topics.tip: In CI, use --tracing=retain-on-failure so traces are only saved when tests fail. The trace viewer shows you exactly what the page looked like at every step — no more 'it works on my machine' debugging.

subject: Selenium WebDriver
description: Selenium architecture, waits, POM, and real-world patterns

topics.title: How Selenium Works
topics.keywords: selenium webdriver architecture HTTP W3C chromedriver geckodriver browser driver protocol
topics.definition: Selenium communicates via HTTP — each command is a separate HTTP request to a driver (chromedriver/geckodriver) which then controls the browser. Three-layer chain.
topics.code:
# Layer stack
# Your test
#   ↕ HTTP (W3C WebDriver protocol)
# Driver (chromedriver / geckodriver)
#   ↕ Native browser API
# Browser (Chrome / Firefox / Edge)

from selenium import webdriver
from selenium.webdriver.common.by import By
from selenium.webdriver.support.ui import WebDriverWait
from selenium.webdriver.support import expected_conditions as EC

# Setup (Selenium 4+ auto-manages driver)
driver = webdriver.Chrome()
driver.get("https://example.com/login")

# You manage waits manually — no auto-wait
wait = WebDriverWait(driver, 10)
btn  = wait.until(EC.element_to_be_clickable(
           (By.ID, "login-btn")
       ))
btn.click()

driver.quit()
topics.tip: Every command in Selenium = one HTTP round trip. This is why Selenium is slower than Playwright. Always use WebDriverWait with Expected Conditions — never Thread.sleep().

topics.title: Locators & StaleElement
topics.keywords: locator By ID CSS XPath name class stale element reference exception re-find
topics.definition: Selenium find_element() queries DOM immediately and returns an element object. If DOM changes before you use it, you get StaleElementReferenceException.
topics.code:
from selenium.webdriver.common.by import By

# Locator strategies
driver.find_element(By.ID,           "username")
driver.find_element(By.CSS_SELECTOR, "#username")
driver.find_element(By.XPATH,        "//input[@id='username']")
driver.find_element(By.NAME,         "username")
driver.find_element(By.CLASS_NAME,   "input-field")
driver.find_element(By.LINK_TEXT,    "Forgot password?")

# Find multiple
elements = driver.find_elements(By.CSS_SELECTOR, ".item")

# StaleElementReferenceException fix — re-find after DOM change
def safe_click(driver, locator):
    for _ in range(3):
        try:
            driver.find_element(*locator).click()
            return
        except StaleElementReferenceException:
            time.sleep(0.5)

# Page Object with @FindBy (cleaner approach)
class LoginPage:
    USERNAME = (By.ID, "username")
    PASSWORD = (By.ID, "password")

    def login(self, u, p):
        self.driver.find_element(*self.USERNAME).send_keys(u)
topics.tip: StaleElementReferenceException means the element was found, then the DOM refreshed (React re-render, SPA navigation). You must re-find the element. Store locator tuples in POM, not WebElement objects.

subject: Appium
description: Appium architecture for Android and iOS mobile testing

topics.title: How Appium Works
topics.keywords: appium architecture server UIAutomator2 XCUITest Android iOS HTTP WebDriver protocol mobile
topics.definition: Appium is a mobile automation server. Your test sends HTTP commands to the Appium Server, which translates them into native platform automation — UIAutomator2 for Android, XCUITest for iOS.
topics.code:
# Layer stack
# Your test (Python / Java / any language)
#   ↕ HTTP (WebDriver protocol)
# Appium Server (Node.js process on port 4723)
#   ↕ Platform driver
# UIAutomator2 (Android) / XCUITest (iOS)
#   ↕
# Real device / Emulator / Simulator

from appium import webdriver
from appium.options import AndroidOptions

options = AndroidOptions()
options.platform_name       = "Android"
options.device_name         = "emulator-5554"
options.app_package         = "com.example.app"
options.app_activity        = ".MainActivity"
options.automation_name     = "UiAutomator2"

driver = webdriver.Remote(
    "http://localhost:4723",
    options=options
)

# Mobile-specific actions
driver.find_element(AppiumBy.ACCESSIBILITY_ID, "login").click()
driver.swipe(500, 1500, 500, 500, 800)  # scroll up
driver.hide_keyboard()
topics.tip: Appium Server must be running before tests start. In CI, start Appium as a background process and add a health-check wait. Android emulator cold boot takes 60-120 seconds — keep it warm in CI using pre-booted snapshots.

topics.title: Desired Capabilities
topics.keywords: desired capabilities platform app package activity device UDID automation name options
topics.definition: Capabilities tell Appium which platform, device, app, and automation driver to use. Wrong capabilities = session creation fails with a cryptic error.
topics.code:
# Android capabilities
android_opts = {
    "platformName":     "Android",
    "platformVersion":  "12",
    "deviceName":       "emulator-5554",
    "appPackage":       "com.example.myapp",
    "appActivity":      ".ui.MainActivity",
    "automationName":   "UiAutomator2",
    "noReset":          True,    # don't reinstall app
    "fullReset":        False,
}

# iOS capabilities
ios_opts = {
    "platformName":     "iOS",
    "platformVersion":  "16.0",
    "deviceName":       "iPhone 14",
    "udid":             "auto",  # simulator UDID
    "app":              "/path/to/app.app",
    "automationName":   "XCUITest",
    "wdaLocalPort":     8100,
}

# Get appPackage from adb (Android)
# adb shell dumpsys window | grep -E 'mCurrentFocus'
topics.tip: Get the correct appPackage and appActivity using adb on a real device or emulator: adb shell dumpsys window | grep mCurrentFocus while the app is open. Wrong activity name is the #1 Appium setup failure.

topics.title: Mutable vs Immutable
topics.keywords: python mutable immutable
topics.definition: Mutable objects can change after creation.
topics.code:
my_list = [1,2]
my_list.append(3)
topics.tip: Tuple immutable, list mutable.

topics.title: Decorators
topics.keywords: python decorators wrapper
topics.definition: Decorators modify function behavior.
topics.code:
def decorator(func):
  pass
topics.tip: Explain wrapper functions clearly.
